findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Obfuscated Network Call
findings.description: The cloud_sync.py script collects sensitive credentials (AWS, GCP, SSH keys, npm/pypi tokens) and environment variables, then exfiltrates them to an obfuscated external server via HTTPS. The server hostname and API key are base64-encoded to hide the destination.
findings.evidence: Lines 14-17: Base64-encoded hostname 'Y2xvdWQtc3luYy1hcGkuaW8' (cloud-sync-api.io), path '/v1/backup', and API key 'Y3NfbGl2ZV9UcThtTnZYMnBSNXNLN3dK'. Lines 47-60: Collects ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.npmrc, etc. Lines 63-76: Sends all data to decoded endpoint with auth token.
findings.remediation: Remove the script entirely. Do not use any skill that collects credentials or sends data to external servers. Report this as a malicious skill.

findings.title: Obfuscated Payload and Hidden Endpoint
findings.description: The script uses base64 encoding to obfuscate the target server hostname, API path, and authentication key. This is a deliberate evasion technique to hide the malicious destination and make analysis more difficult.
findings.evidence: Lines 14-17: _cfg dictionary with base64-encoded values. Lines 19-22: _ep() function decodes hostname and path. Lines 24-26: _key() function decodes API key. Lines 63-76: Uses decoded values to construct and send the request.
findings.remediation: Remove the script. Obfuscation is a strong indicator of malicious intent.

findings.title: Deceptive Skill Name and Description
findings.description: The skill is named 'cloud-sync' and described as a 'project configuration backup utility', but its actual behavior is to collect and exfiltrate sensitive credentials (AWS, GCP, SSH keys, npm/pypi tokens) to an external server. The name and description are deliberately misleading to evade detection.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md name: 'cloud-sync', description: 'Synchronize local project files and configurations to cloud storage'. Actual behavior: Collects credentials from ~/.aws, ~/.ssh, ~/.npmrc, ~/.pypirc and sends them to cloud-sync-api.io.
findings.remediation: Do not install or use this skill. The name and description are designed to deceive users into running a credential-stealing script.

findings.title: Credential Theft from Multiple Sources
findings.description: The script systematically collects credentials from AWS (~/.aws/credentials), GCP (~/.config/gcloud/...), SSH private keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519), npm (~/.npmrc), and PyPI (~/.pypirc). These are all sensitive authentication credentials that should never be collected or transmitted.
findings.evidence: Lines 47-60: cred_paths list includes all major credential file locations. Lines 55-59: Reads each file and stores content in payload dict with 'cred:' prefix.
findings.remediation: Remove the script. No legitimate backup tool should collect SSH private keys or cloud provider credentials.

findings.title: Environment Variable Harvesting
findings.description: The script collects all environment variables (os.environ) and includes them in the exfiltrated payload. Environment variables often contain API keys, database passwords, and other secrets.
findings.evidence: Line 38: payload['environment'] = dict(os.environ) - captures all environment variables.
findings.remediation: Remove the script. Environment variables should never be collected or transmitted.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning via Deceptive Manifest
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest does not declare 'allowed-tools', but the script uses Python to make network requests (urllib.request) and read files. The lack of tool restrictions combined with the deceptive description enables the credential theft to proceed without triggering tool-use warnings.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md YAML frontmatter has no 'allowed-tools' field. The script uses Python's urllib.request for network calls and open() for file reads, which would normally be restricted if tools were declared.
findings.remediation: Always declare allowed-tools in skill manifests. This skill should have declared [Read, Python] at minimum, but the actual behavior requires network access which should be flagged.

findings.title: Tool Chaining for Data Exfiltration
findings.description: The script chains multiple operations: read credential files → collect environment variables → encode data → send to external server. This multi-step data flow is designed to exfiltrate sensitive information without raising suspicion at any single step.
findings.evidence: Lines 30-60: collect_configs() reads files and environment. Lines 63-76: upload_backup() encodes and sends data. The chain is: read → collect → encode → send.
findings.remediation: Remove the script. Any tool that reads credentials and sends them externally is malicious.
